{{神社| 名称=湯谷神社| 所在地=滋賀県米原市米原771| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|}}| 祭神=大己貴命| 社格=村社| 創建=創祀年代不詳| 例祭=[[5月10日]]| 神事=}}
'''湯谷神社'''（ゆたにじんじゃ）は[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]にある[[神社]]である。
==ご祭神== * [[大己貴命]] * [[水門神]] * [[保食神]]
==ご神紋== * 六ッ槌
==歴史== :創祀年代は不詳である。古来六所権現と称したが、明治以後地名に因み、湯谷神社と改称した。上古出雲国人が諸国を巡ってこの地に至り、里人に池を掘らせると、霊泉が湧き出、又荒地を開墾したところ、五穀がよく成育したので、出雲の祖神大己貴命を小谷の岩上に奉斎した。温泉は万病に効くと伝えられ、崇敬者が多く、後に水門神、保食神を合祀したと伝えられている。輿地志略に「湯谷は昔此の地に温泉あり諸病を治す。或日葦毛の子を此の湯坪に洗いしより此の湯かるゝ」とある。後白河上皇の御代、日吉の社領となって、当社を山王権現社とも称せられた。今井備中守秀遠は、箕浦庄の地頭職として、当地を治めた故、当社を守護神と崇め、その一族米原氏も社殿を修理し、社領を献じた。文明3年米原山太尾城に激戦があり、当社は焼失したが、天文13年今井氏は、米原氏をして再興せしめ、大山咋命を勧請した。文禄4年岩脇氏は、社殿を修復し神門を寄進した。享保17年彦根藩主井伊直惟は社殿を再興し、手水鉢、木造狛犬を献じ、明治22年には、三条実美が社号額を寄せている。又境内には明治11年明治天皇北陸巡幸の際小休止された北村邸の行在所がある。大正7年村社に列し、同年神饌幣帛料供進指定となる。
==祭事== * 例祭 10月第2日曜 - 曳山子ども狂言
==境内社== * 前光稲荷神社 * 六所神社 * 牛頭天王社 * 金比羅神社 * 春川稲荷神社
==アクセス== * JR[[東海道本線]]・[[東海道新幹線]][[米原駅]]下車、東口より徒歩5分
== 関連項目 == *[[神道]] *[[神社]]

==ご祭神== * [[大己貴命]] * [[水門神]] * [[保食神]]
==ご神紋== * 六ッ槌
==歴史== :創祀年代は不詳である。古来六所権現と称したが、明治以後地名に因み、湯谷神社と改称した。上古出雲国人が諸国を巡ってこの地に至り、里人に池を掘らせると、霊泉が湧き出、又荒地を開墾したところ、五穀がよく成育したので、出雲の祖神大己貴命を小谷の岩上に奉斎した。温泉は万病に効くと伝えられ、崇敬者が多く、後に水門神、保食神を合祀したと伝えられている。輿地志略に「湯谷は昔此の地に温泉あり諸病を治す。或日葦毛の子を此の湯坪に洗いしより此の湯かるゝ」とある。後白河上皇の御代、日吉の社領となって、当社を山王権現社とも称せられた。今井備中守秀遠は、箕浦庄の地頭職として、当地を治めた故、当社を守護神と崇め、その一族米原氏も社殿を修理し、社領を献じた。文明3年米原山太尾城に激戦があり、当社は焼失したが、天文13年今井氏は、米原氏をして再興せしめ、大山咋命を勧請した。文禄4年岩脇氏は、社殿を修復し神門を寄進した。享保17年彦根藩主井伊直惟は社殿を再興し、手水鉢、木造狛犬を献じ、明治22年には、三条実美が社号額を寄せている。又境内には明治11年明治天皇北陸巡幸の際小休止された北村邸の行在所がある。大正7年村社に列し、同年神饌幣帛料供進指定となる。
==祭事== * 例祭 10月第2日曜 - 曳山子ども狂言
==境内社== * 前光稲荷神社 * 六所神社 * 牛頭天王社 * 金比羅神社 * 春川稲荷神社
==アクセス== * JR[[東海道本線]]・[[東海道新幹線]][[米原駅]]下車、東口より徒歩5分

{{Shinto Shrine | Name = Yuya Shrine | Image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | Location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | Coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | Shrine Rank = Village Shrine | Founded = Date unknown | Annual Festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined {{ill|Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}}. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== Annual Festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{Shinto Shrine | Name = Yuya Shrine | Image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | Location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | Coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | Shrine Rank = Village Shrine | Founded = Date unknown | Annual Festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined {{ill|Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}}. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== Annual Festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined {{ill|Ōyamazumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}}. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== Annual Festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Yuya Shrine | image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.
== References == {{節stub}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} *

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}{{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|ja|5月10日}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province }} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province }} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2016年6月13日 (月) 20:02 (UTC)}} {{神社 | 名称=湯谷神社 | 画像=[[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | 所在地=滋賀県米原市米原771 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=大己貴命 | 社格=村社 | 創建=創祀年代不詳 | 例祭=[[5月10日]] | 神事= }}
'''湯谷神社'''（ゆたにじんじゃ）は[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]にある[[神社]]である。
==ご祭神== * [[大己貴命]] * [[水門神]] * [[保食神]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
==ご神紋== * 左三ッ巴 * 六ッ槌<ref name=":0" />
==歴史== :創祀年代は不詳である。古来六所権現と称したが、明治以後地名に因み、湯谷神社と改称した。上古出雲国人が諸国を巡ってこの地に至り、里人に池を掘らせると、霊泉が湧き出、又荒地を開墾したところ、五穀がよく成育したので、出雲の祖神大己貴命を小谷の岩上に奉斎した。温泉は万病に効くと伝えられ、崇敬者が多く、後に水門神、保食神を合祀したと伝えられている。輿地志略に「湯谷は昔此の地に温泉あり諸病を治す。或日葦毛の子を此の湯坪に洗いしより此の湯かるゝ」とある。後白河上皇の御代、日吉の社領となって、当社を山王権現社とも称せられた。今井備中守秀遠は、箕浦庄の地頭職として、当地を治めた故、当社を守護神と崇め、その一族米原氏も社殿を修理し、社領を献じた。文明3年米原山太尾城に激戦があり、当社は焼失したが、天文13年今井氏は、米原氏をして再興せしめ、大山咋命を勧請した。文禄4年岩脇氏は、社殿を修復し神門を寄進した。享保17年彦根藩主井伊直惟は社殿を再興し、手水鉢、木造狛犬を献じ、明治22年には、三条実美が社号額を寄せている。又境内には明治11年明治天皇北陸巡幸の際小休止された北村邸の行在所がある。大正7年村社に列し、同年神饌幣帛料供進指定となる。<ref name=":0" />
==祭事== * 例祭 10月第2日曜 - 曳山子ども狂言
==境内社== * 前光稲荷神社 * 六所神社 * 牛頭天王社 * 金比羅神社 * 春川稲荷神社<ref name=":0" />
==アクセス== * JR[[東海道本線]]・[[東海道新幹線]][[米原駅]]下車、東口より徒歩5分
== 脚注 == {{節stub}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[神道]]
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ゆたにしんしや}} [[Category:滋賀県の神社]] [[Category:滋賀県の旧村社]] [[Category:米原市の歴史]] [[Category:米原市の建築物]]

==境内社== * 前光稲荷神社 * 六所神社 * 牛頭天王社 * 金比羅神社 * 春川稲荷神社<ref name=":0" />

Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|ja|保食神|1=Ukemochi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukemochi|qq=}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|ja|米原駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maibara Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|ja|東海道本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō Main Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Tōkaidō Shinkansen|ja|東海道新幹線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō Shinkansen|qq=}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q128845623]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]][[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]] Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = [[:en:May 10|May 10]] }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yutani-jinja (Maibara)]][[ja:湯谷神社 (米原市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q128845623]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]][[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]] Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = [[:en:May 10|May 10]] }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.
