'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Maibara, Shiga|Maibara]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi-no-Kami]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from [[:en:Maibara Station|Maibara Station]] on the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] and [[:en:Tokaido Shinkansen|Tōkaidō Shinkansen]]. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yutani-jinja (Maibara)]][[ja:湯谷神社 (米原市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yutani-jinja (Maibara)]][[ja:湯谷神社 (米原市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yutani-jinja (Maibara)]][[ja:湯谷神社 (米原市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q128845623]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|en|May 10|WD=Q2557|lt=May 10}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|en|Maibara Station|WD=Q801158|lt=Maibara Station}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} and {{ill|Tokaido Shinkansen|en|Tokaido Shinkansen|WD=Q660895|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.
== References ==
{{Reflist}} *

{{Infobox religious building| | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara|1=Maibara, Shiga}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|en|May 10|WD=Q2557|lt=May 10|1=May 10}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara|1=Maibara, Shiga}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|1=Ukemochi}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|en|Maibara Station|WD=Q801158|lt=Maibara Station|1=Maibara Station}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} and {{ill|Tokaido Shinkansen|en|Tokaido Shinkansen|WD=Q660895|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.
== References ==

{{Infobox religious building| | name = Yuya Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Maibara yuya shrine.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara|1=Maibara, Shiga}} Maibara 771 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|18|46|N|136|17|43|E|region:JP-25_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} | type = Village Shrine | consecration_year = Date unknown | festival = {{ill|May 10|en|May 10|WD=Q2557|lt=May 10|1=May 10}} }}
'''Yuya Shrine''' (湯谷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Maibara, Shiga|en|Maibara, Shiga|WD=Q824020|lt=Maibara|1=Maibara, Shiga}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Mikado-no-Kami|ja|水門神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikado-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|1=Ukemochi}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=滋賀県神社誌|date=1987-12-20|publisher=滋賀県神社庁|pages=449,450}}</ref>
== Crest == * Left-facing Mitsudomoe * Rokutsuchi (Six Mallets)<ref name=":0" />
== History == The founding date of the shrine is unknown. Historically referred to as the Rokusho Gongen, it was renamed Yuya Shrine during the Meiji era after the local area. According to tradition, a group from {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} reached this area in ancient times, dug a pond, and discovered sacred spring water. They cultivated the surrounding land, which yielded abundant crops. To honor these blessings, they enshrined the ancestral deity of Izumo, {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, on a rock in Odani. The hot springs were believed to cure all ailments, attracting many worshippers. Later, Mikado-no-Kami and Ukemochi-no-Kami were also enshrined.
Historical texts such as the ''Kojichiryaku'' mention that “Yuya once had hot springs believed to heal various diseases.” During the reign of Emperor Go-Shirakawa, the shrine became part of the Hie shrine estate and was also referred to as Sannō Gongen Shrine. Imai Bitchū-no-Kami Hidefuyu, a local ruler, revered the shrine as a guardian deity. His clan, the Maibara family, repaired the shrine and donated lands.
In 1471 (Bunmei 3), a fierce battle at Maibara San-Taobyō Castle led to the shrine’s destruction. In 1544 (Tenbun 13), the Imai clan tasked the Maibara family with its restoration, and they enshrined [[Ōyamatsumi]]. In 1595 (Bunroku 4), the Iwawaki family repaired the shrine and donated a sacred gate. In 1732 (Kyōhō 17), Ii Naotaka, lord of Hikone Domain, restored the shrine, donated a ''temizu bachi'' (water basin), and wooden guardian lion-dog statues. In 1889 (Meiji 22), Sanjō Sanetomi gifted the shrine with its name plaque. The shrine grounds also contain the site of a temporary imperial lodge used during Emperor Meiji’s tour of Hokuriku in 1878 (Meiji 11).
In 1918 (Taishō 7), the shrine was designated as a Village Shrine, and its offerings of imperial foods and rituals were officially established.<ref name=":0" />
== festival == * Second Sunday of October - Hiki-yama Children's Kyōgen (traditional puppet performance)
== Auxiliary Shrines == * Maemitsu Inari Shrine * Rokusho Shrine * Gozu Tennō Shrine * Konpira Shrine * Harukawa Inari Shrine<ref name=":0" />
== Access == * By Train: A 5-minute walk east from {{ill|Maibara Station|en|Maibara Station|WD=Q801158|lt=Maibara Station|1=Maibara Station}} on the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} and {{ill|Tokaido Shinkansen|en|Tokaido Shinkansen|WD=Q660895|lt=Tōkaidō Shinkansen|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}. * By Car: The shrine is a short walk from the east exit of Maibara Station.
== References ==
{{Reflist}} *
